Summary of what was found: Thin-wall injection molding is described as packaging with wall thickness often below 1 mm; it is positioned as a lightweight, precise, cost-efficient route and is explicitly linked to cosmetic jars, lipstick tubes, and compact cases.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: thin-wall injection molding. Packaging format relevance: cosmetic jars, lipstick tubes, compact cases, and rigid beauty components. Weight/material reduction evidence: thin-wall molding reduces plastic usage and can shorten cooling times while supporting complex shapes. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: supports rigid beauty packs as realistic thin-wall candidates rather than purely food-packaging transfers. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market rigid components, with premium use possible if finish and haptics are maintained. Performance/limitation notes: equipment-supplier technical overview, not a pack-by-pack commercial case study. Use in screening: define thin-wall molding as a viable process route for cream jars, lipstick tubes, and compact cases.
Source name: Thin Wall Injection Molding for Consumer Packaging: Trends & Innovations — YIZUMI — https://www.yizumi.com/en/news/product/industry-trend/thin-wall-injection-molding-for-consumer-packaging-trends-innovations

Summary of what was found: Graham’s AccuStrength technology uses targeted material placement to make containers up to 15% lighter while retaining structural integrity.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: targeted material placement for rigid bottles. Packaging format relevance: primary rigid bottles, especially oval or rectangular plastic bottles. Weight/material reduction evidence: up to 15% lighter while retaining structural integrity. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: viable for PET/HDPE-style cosmetics and personal-care bottles where grams can be removed from the body while preserving performance. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market and high-volume personal-care bottles; possible masstige use if appearance is unchanged. Performance/limitation notes: source does not publish exact cosmetic SKUs or full pack-weight data. Use in screening: shortlist for rigid-bottle lightweighting without changing pack size or overall format.
Source name: Graham Packaging Optimizes Lightweighting and Sustainability with AccuStrength Technology — Graham Packaging — https://www.grahampackaging.com/insights/graham-packaging-optimizes-lightweighting-and-sustainability-with-accustrength-technology/

Summary of what was found: Amcor’s Quantum PET finish technology removes more than 50% of the material and weight from the bottle finish while maintaining a premium look and feel and allowing up to 100% recycled material use.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: lightweight PET neck/finish technology. Packaging format relevance: PET bottles and bottle-finish/thread areas. Weight/material reduction evidence: more than 50% material and weight removed from the bottle finish. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: supports a same-function, less-resin route for personal-care PET bottles, especially where finish weight is significant. Premium versus mass-market fit: relevant to masstige or prestige PET packs because the source says premium look and feel can be maintained. Performance/limitation notes: focused on finish optimization, not a full-bottle weight reduction figure. Use in screening: include neck/finish optimization as a separate lightweighting lever, not just bottle-body downgauging.
Source name: Lightweighting Technology Launches Quantum Leap for Packaging — Amcor — https://www.amcor.com/media/news/lightweighting-technology-quantum

Summary of what was found: ENGEL states material can account for up to 70% of total cost of ownership in thin-wall molding; reducing wall thickness to 0.4 mm cut part weight/material use by 20% to 21.56 g, with cycle times below 2 seconds.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: ultra-thin-wall injection molding. Packaging format relevance: PP tubs, jars, pails, lids, and high-volume rigid packs. Weight/material reduction evidence: 20% part weight/material reduction to 21.56 g in an example; material can represent up to 70% of TCO; cycle times below 2 seconds are possible. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: supports high-volume beauty and personal-care tubs/jars where small gram savings scale across continuous production. Premium versus mass-market fit: core mass-market scalability source. Performance/limitation notes: example is food pail rather than cosmetics; high injection speed, consistency, and tight machine control are needed to avoid rejects. Use in screening: treat as evidence that thin-wall PP can be economically compelling but process-sensitive.
Source name: Reduced wall-thickness for thin-walled packaging: From the thin-wall to the ultra-thin-wall pail — ENGEL — https://www.engelglobal.com/en/blog/reduced-wall-thickness-for-thin-wall-packaging

Summary of what was found: SABIC’s thin-wall packaging platform highlights good flow behavior, fast crystallization, top-load performance, down-gauging possibilities, and lower cost via shorter production cycles for PP and PE packaging.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: high-flow PP/PE resins for thin-wall packaging. Packaging format relevance: PP jars/tubs, lids, caps, closures, and stackable packaging. Weight/material reduction evidence: supports downgauging through flow, crystallization, top-load performance, and shorter cycles. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: high-flow PP/PE grades are foundational for mass-market thin-wall conversion in beauty packaging. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market; selected premium secondary/internal parts possible. Performance/limitation notes: supplier overview and does not provide pack-specific gram savings. Use in screening: use as material-enabler evidence for thin-wall PP/PE rigid components.

Summary of what was found: Trexel’s P-Series MuCell thin-wall packaging system reports 3% material reduction on a 6 oz cup and enables thinner base and sidewalls while keeping a thicker sealing rim, with less resin use and lower energy consumption.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: MuCell microcellular foaming for thin-wall packaging. Packaging format relevance: tubs, refill cups, lids, and selected plastic secondary components. Weight/material reduction evidence: 3% material reduction on a 6 oz cup and selective thinning of base/sidewalls while preserving a thicker sealing rim. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: useful where reducing resin mass is important but local rigidity/sealing areas must be preserved. Premium versus mass-market fit: mass-market and selected premium internal/refill components if surface quality passes. Performance/limitation notes: examples are not beauty packs; premium-surface acceptance needs validation. Use in screening: shortlist as a process/material-structure route where standard wall thinning would weaken functional areas.
Source name: Packaging Molding with Thin Walls / P-300 SCF Delivery System — Trexel — https://trexel.com/industries/packaging-molding/ ; https://trexel.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Trexel_P-300_datasheet_2019_FINAL.pdf

Summary of what was found: Amcor’s Function of Beauty case used 100% premium-grade rPET for custom shampoo and conditioner bottles while maintaining consumer experience and saving about 350,000 pounds of virgin resin annually.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: 100% rPET beauty bottles. Packaging format relevance: shampoo and conditioner bottles. Weight/material reduction evidence: source focuses on virgin resin displacement rather than explicit wall-gauge thinning; PET/rPET remains a lightweight shatter-resistant bottle route compared with heavier materials. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: direct beauty-sector example that rPET bottles can preserve consumer experience. Premium versus mass-market fit: mass-market and masstige; possible premium where rPET appearance meets requirements. Performance/limitation notes: recycled-content evidence rather than explicit downgauging. Use in screening: keep rPET bottles as a commercially acceptable beauty-pack route alongside thin-wall PET work.
Source name: Amcor designed the bottle, the consumer designs the shampoo — Amcor — https://www.amcor.com/insights/case-studies/function-of-beauty

Summary of what was found: Neopac’s Eco-Stripped Tube uses 30% less plastic than standard versions; it builds on a lightweight tube with sleeve wall thickness reduced from 0.5 mm to 0.35 mm without sacrificing product protection, haptics, esthetics, or key qualities.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: lightweight/downgauged tubes. Packaging format relevance: PE, PP, and coextruded skincare, sunscreen, hand cream, and personal-care tubes. Weight/material reduction evidence: 30% less plastic and sleeve wall reduction from 0.5 mm to 0.35 mm. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: direct commercial tube lightweighting relevant to beauty and personal care. Premium versus mass-market fit: useful for both; valuable for premium caution analysis because haptics and aesthetics are explicitly addressed. Performance/limitation notes: no detailed filling-line or formula matrix provided. Use in screening: shortlist downgauged tubes as a core route where consumer pack size remains unchanged.
Source name: Neopac Introduces Eco-Stripped Tube with 30% Less Plastics, Including Slimmed-Down Shoulder & Cap — Hoffmann Neopac — https://www.hoffmann-neopac.com/hu/hirek-esemenyek/neopac-introduces-eco-stripped-tube-with-30-less-plastics-including-slimmed-down-shoulder-cap

Summary of what was found: Albéa’s EcoFusion Top is a two-in-one solution that delivers more than 80% weight reduction in the top solution and about 55% weight reduction for the whole tube versus standard tubes.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: lightweight tube top/shoulder/closure architecture. Packaging format relevance: cosmetic and personal-care tubes. Weight/material reduction evidence: more than 80% top-solution weight reduction and around 55% whole-tube weight reduction versus standard tubes. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: shows that tube head/cap systems can hold large plastic-saving potential beyond sleeve thickness. Premium versus mass-market fit: strong for mass-market and premium if decoration/formula/filling compatibility are validated. Performance/limitation notes: proprietary design and may not fit every decoration, formula, or filling combination. Use in screening: evaluate tubes by sleeve plus shoulder/cap/top system, not sleeve gauge alone.
Source name: Albéa’s EcoFusion Top – unrivalled weight reduction — Albéa — https://www.albea-group.com/article/market-first-albeas-ecofusion-top-unrivalled-weight-reduction

Summary of what was found: Aptar’s Disc Top Lite closure is 23% lighter than a standard alternative and is positioned as combining lightweight efficiency with a premium look and feel.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: lightweight disc-top closure. Packaging format relevance: shampoo, body wash, lotion, and personal-care bottles. Weight/material reduction evidence: 23% lighter than Aptar’s standard closure alternative. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: closure grams can be reduced without visibly degrading appearance. Premium versus mass-market fit: especially relevant for masstige and premium brands because premium look and feel are preserved. Performance/limitation notes: comparison is against Aptar’s standard closure, not a market-wide benchmark. Use in screening: create a closure-lightweighting workstream alongside bottle-body and tube-body lightweighting.
Source name: 2 in. Disc Top Lite | 24mm Custom Thread, Logo Insertable Closure — Aptar — https://aptar.com/en-us/products/2-disc-top-lite-closure

Summary of what was found: Aptar’s Minia flexible-pouch fitment is a one-piece, 100% polyethylene closure for beauty and personal care pouches; it weighs less than 1 gram, is PCR-capable, and is ISTA 6 e-commerce capable.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: ultra-light one-piece PE pouch fitment. Packaging format relevance: mini pouches, samples, refills, and small flexible packs. Weight/material reduction evidence: fitment weighs less than 1 gram and is mono-PE. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: important for samples, mini sizes, sachet-to-pouch upgrades, and refill formats. Premium versus mass-market fit: high-priority for mass-market and refill/sample programs; premium use possible for trial sizes. Performance/limitation notes: intended pack size range is 7–50 ml, so not a direct substitute for full-size rigid bottles or jars. Use in screening: prioritize for small-format and refill applications where closure weight and material simplification matter.
Source name: Minia One-Piece Flexible Fitment Closure for Mini Pouches — Aptar — https://aptar.com/en-us/products/minia-flexible-fitment-closure

Summary of what was found: Dow’s INNATE TH60 resin is positioned for PE-film downgauging by improving toughness without compromising stiffness; it offers flex-crack resistance and supports packaging designed for recyclability.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: downgauged PE flexible films enabled by tough/stiff resin. Packaging format relevance: mono-PE refill packs, reserve packs, sachets, and pouches. Weight/material reduction evidence: supports downgauging through toughness and flex-crack resistance rather than simple thinning. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: mass-market flexible refills and pouches for beauty/personal care. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest mass-market route; potential premium refill use. Performance/limitation notes: resin data page rather than end-pack case study. Use in screening: flexible downgauging must be tied to toughness/crack resistance, not thinner gauge alone.
Source name: INNATE TH60 Precision Packaging Resin — Dow — https://www.dow.com/en-us/pdp.innate-th60-precision-packaging-resin.453388z.html

Summary of what was found: Mondi reduced a coextruded barrier film structure from the usual 66–70 microns to 50 microns, resulting in about 25% less material and lower transport weight.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: downgauged coextruded barrier film. Packaging format relevance: barrier flexible packs, pouches, and refill formats. Weight/material reduction evidence: thickness reduced to 50 microns from 66–70 microns, creating about 25% less material and lower transport weight. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: supports exploration of downgauged pouches/refills where barrier remains necessary. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market refill and pouch formats. Performance/limitation notes: food/bag-in-box example, so fragrance, oil, and cosmetic formula-specific barrier requirements need testing. Use in screening: include downgauged barrier films as a route where barrier engineering can preserve function while cutting material.
Source name: Mondi continues to innovate with sustainable solutions, with next generation of Bag-in-Box technical films — Mondi — https://www.mondigroup.com/news-and-insight/2018/mondi-continues-to-innovate-with-sustainable-solutions-with-next-generation-of-bag-in-box-technical-films/

Summary of what was found: APR’s PET Thermoform Packaging Design Resource says PET thermoforms can be designed to be compatible with the PET bottle stream; key considerations include clear PET with specified IV and recognized PET-compatible labels, inks, and adhesives.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: PET thermoform design for recycling. Packaging format relevance: thermoformed PET trays, insert pods, display components, and thin-gauge PET parts. Weight/material reduction evidence: design-for-recycling source rather than mechanical lightweighting case study; relevant because material-efficient thermoforms must also maintain recyclability. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: secondary trays, inserts, and display components in beauty. Premium versus mass-market fit: both, especially for transparent trays and inserts. Performance/limitation notes: not a weight-reduction case study. Use in screening: apply as recyclability screen for PET tray/insert routes so downgauging does not create end-of-life issues.
Source name: PET Thermoform Packaging Design Resource Document — Association of Plastic Recyclers — https://plasticsrecycling.org/documents/res-pet-01/

Summary of what was found: Nefab describes thermoforming as a cost-effective process for lightweight packaging; trays, cushions, inserts, clamshells, and inner fittings can be made in thin to heavy gauge and with up to 100% recycled content.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: thermoformed protective packaging. Packaging format relevance: secondary and tertiary plastic components such as trays, cushions, inserts, clamshells, and inner fittings. Weight/material reduction evidence: thermoforming is positioned as cost-effective and lightweight; materials include HDPE, PET, ABS, and PP and can include up to 100% recycled content. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: protective trays, shipping inserts, and durable inner fittings for beauty. Premium versus mass-market fit: both, depending on finish and fragility needs. Performance/limitation notes: focused on protective packaging rather than consumer-facing primary packs. Use in screening: use thermoforming where injection molding would be unnecessarily heavy or expensive.

Summary of what was found: A Packaging World report on Colgate/Polyplastics shows extrusion blow molding of HDPE/COC achieved about 15% weight reduction, but further reduction caused drop-test failures; injection stretch blow molding then reached 30% reduction with suitable blends.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: HDPE/COC bottle lightweighting and process comparison. Packaging format relevance: blow-molded HDPE bottles and rigid liquid containers. Weight/material reduction evidence: about 15% reduction in extrusion blow molding before drop failures; up to 30% reduction through injection stretch blow molding with suitable blends. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: useful for HDPE personal-care bottles and advanced bottle process routes. Premium versus mass-market fit: mass-market and selected personal-care formats. Performance/limitation notes: strong caution source showing over-downgauging can fail drop testing; household-packaging example and COC modification are not direct off-the-shelf beauty solutions. Use in screening: tie downgauging decisions to process route, resin behavior, and drop testing.
Source name: When COC Meets HDPE: How Colgate Pushed a Familiar Resin Into New Territory — Packaging World — https://www.packworld.com/rigid/containers-closures/article/22960681/when-coc-meets-hdpe-how-colgate-pushed-a-familiar-resin-into-new-territory

Summary of what was found: Dow states liquid-packaging materials can be downgauged while maintaining durability and toughness, and links downgauging to drop performance, reduced leak rates, smoother processing, and faster filling.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: flexible liquid-packaging materials for downgauging. Packaging format relevance: flexible refill pouches and liquid personal-care packs. Weight/material reduction evidence: downgauging tied to durability, toughness, drop performance, leak rate, processing, and filling speed. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: supports flexible personal-care refill packs where leak/drop/fill performance is critical. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market refill packs; selected premium refill systems possible. Performance/limitation notes: food/liquid-packaging oriented, so cosmetic formula compatibility requires separate validation. Use in screening: flexible downgauging must be judged by seal integrity, leak rate, drop resistance, and filling performance alongside gram reduction.
Source name: Spill-Resistant Liquid Packaging Material Solutions — Dow — https://www.dow.com/en-us/market/mkt-packaging/sub-pkg-flexible-packaging/app-pkg-flexible-pkg-food/app4-pkg-flexible-pkg-food-liquid.html

Summary of what was found: Amcor/Berry says sustainable stretch-film design depends on recycled content, material downgauging, and recyclability, but warns recycled materials can reduce stretchability and may require careful thickness selection to maintain performance.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: downgauged tertiary stretch film with recycled content. Packaging format relevance: pallet stretch wrap and distribution films. Weight/material reduction evidence: material downgauging is a stated sustainability lever, but thickness may need adjustment when recycled content affects stretchability. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: tertiary logistics films for beauty distribution. Premium versus mass-market fit: mass-market and distribution operations across all segments. Performance/limitation notes: about stretch film rather than primary or consumer-facing packs. Use in screening: caution that thinner plus recycled does not automatically mean lower total material use if performance loss forces greater thickness.
Source name: Selecting High-Performance, Sustainable Stretch Films – Key Considerations — Amcor Packaging Solutions / Berry Global content hosted by Amcor — https://packagingsolutions.amcor.com/en/news/articles/selecting-high-performance-sustainable-stretch-films
